does: This paper introduces AI-NNC, an open-source modular framework that enables unified agentic AI control across heterogeneous network infrastructure spanning radio access networks, optical transport, and core domains. The framework adopts a protocol-agnostic architecture where physical devices connect through lightweight Python adapters while control logic executes via domain-specific applications. Beyond direct closed-loop control, AI-NNC supports dataset collection, agentic AI experimentation, and coordinated testbed orchestration through standardized control and measurement interfaces, addressing the fragmentation of existing domain-specific controllers like O-RAN RIC that lack native multi-domain coordination capabilities.
connects: This work extends prior research on network automation and AI-driven control systems by tackling the persistent problem of domain-specific network controllers that operate in isolation. It builds on foundational work in Software-Defined Networking and network slicing that demonstrated the value of programmable, unified network control, while responding to recent advances in agentic AI systems that demand greater integration across infrastructure boundaries. The framework synthesizes insights from O-RAN initiatives, optical network automation, and core network management, recognizing that 6G systems require orchestration beyond what existing siloed controllers can provide and responding to the broader challenge of coordinating heterogeneous AI agents across distributed systems.
enables: AI-NNC establishes infrastructure for future research into safe multi-domain network orchestration and agentic AI autonomy in telecommunications. By providing unified measurement and control interfaces, it enables experiments in cross-domain optimization, resource scheduling, and fault recovery that were previously difficult to coordinate. The framework supports development and validation of more sophisticated AI control policies, federated learning across network domains, and exploration of safety mechanisms for agentic systems in critical infrastructure. It positions future work toward autonomous 6G network management, discovery of emergent coordination patterns, and systematic study of how to maintain safety guarantees when multiple AI agents operate across tightly-coupled physical domains.
